title: Bayes’ Theorem Flu Test Example
text: Bayes’ Theorem foundational concept probability, statistics, machine learning. healthcare analytics, frequently used update clinical risk estimates new patient data becomes available. vignette walks realistic example using influenza testing data compute: probability flu given positive test result full 2×2 contingency table accessible visualizations generated bayes() function

title: Bayes’ Theorem Flu Test Example
text: Public health data provide following estimates: Prevalence flu population: P()=0.07P() = 0.07 Sensitivity flu test (true positive rate): P(B∣)=0.92P(B \mid ) = 0.92 Specificity test: P(B−∣−)=0.93P(B^- \mid ^-) = 0.93, implies false positive rate P(B∣−)=0.07P(B \mid ^-) = 0.07 Given detail, probability actually flu patient tests positive?

title: Bayes’ Theorem Flu Test Example
text: value represents: P(∣B)P(\mid B), probability flu given positive test.

title: Bayes’ Theorem Flu Test Example
text: table shows scaled counts (default population size = 1000): True Positives False Positives False Negatives True Negatives
code: result$contingency_table ##          Result Condition_Positive Condition_Negative  Total ## 1 Test Positive               64.4               65.1  129.5 ## 2 Test Negative                5.6              864.9  870.5 ## 3         Total               70.0              930.0 1000.0

title: Bayes’ Theorem Flu Test Example
text: Bayes’ Theorem calculations can performed manually validation results: Given P()=0.07P() = 0.07 P(−)=1−P()=0.93P(^-) = 1 - P() = 0.93 P(B∣)=0.92P(B \mid ) = 0.92 P(B∣−)=0.07P(B \mid ^-) = 0.07 Compute probability positive test Calculate P(B)P(B), probability positive test, calculated using Law Total Probability Bayes’ Theorem, compute P(∣B)P(\mid B), probability flu given positive test result

title: Introduction to bayesToolkit
text: bayesToolkit provides implementation Bayes’ Theorem, including: input normalization prevalence, sensitivity, false positive rates scaled contingency table three color-impaired-safe visualizations clean, reproducible workflow

title: Introduction to bayesToolkit
code: library(bayesToolkit)  result <- bayes(7, 92, 7)  result$posterior_probability result$contingency_table result$barchart result$heatmap result$posterior_plot

title: Introduction to bayesToolkit
text: function accepts multiple numeric formats inputs validated converted decimal probabilities. decimals(.07) whole numbers (7) percentage strings (“7%”)

title: Introduction to bayesToolkit
text: function returns three ggplot2 objects using Okabe-Ito palette accessibility: grouped barchart heatmap posterior probability bar plot

what: Bayesian Probability Computation and Visualization Tools for R
title: Tools for Bayes' Theorem Calculations
text: bayesToolkit provides streamlined interface computing Bayes’ Theorem, generating contingency tables, producing clear visualizations support analytical workflows, decision modeling, probabilistic reasoning. package lightweight, dependency‑free, designed clarity, reproducibility, operational use.

title: Tools for Bayes' Theorem Calculations
text: Compute posterior probabilities using Bayes’ Theorem Generate 2×2 contingency tables prevalence, sensitivity, false‑positive rates Grouped bar charts Heatmaps Posterior probability plots Simple, transparent API suitable analytics, modeling, reporting

title: Tools for Bayes' Theorem Calculations
text: package stored inside subdirectory larger GitHub repository. Install using devtools::install_github() subdir argument.
code: install.packages("devtools")    # required if not already on system  devtools::install_github(       # installation code   "mcvanderbilt/code-library",   subdir = "r/bayesToolkit" )  library(bayesToolkit)           # load the package

title: Tools for Bayes' Theorem Calculations
code: result <- bayes(   prevalence     = 0.07,   sensitivity    = 0.92,   false_positive = 0.07 )  # Posterior probability result$posterior_probability  # Contingency table result$contingency_table  # Visualizations result$barchart result$heatmap result$posterior_plot

what: Bayes' Theorem Calculator with Color‑Impaired‑Safe Visualizations Computes the posterior probability \(P(A|B)\) using Bayes' Theorem, generates a contingency table scaled to a user‑defined population size, and produces three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations: a grouped bar chart, a heatmap, and a posterior probability bar plot. — bayes
title: Bayes' Theorem Calculator with Color‑Impaired‑Safe Visualizations Computes the posterior probability \(P(A|B)\) using Bayes' Theorem, generates a contingency table scaled to a user‑defined population size, and produces three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations: a grouped bar chart, a heatmap, and a posterior probability bar plot. — bayes
text: function accepts prevalence, sensitivity, false positive rate decimals, whole numbers, percentage strings (e.g., "7%", "92", "0.07"). inputs validated normalized decimal probabilities.

title: Bayes' Theorem Calculator with Color‑Impaired‑Safe Visualizations Computes the posterior probability \(P(A|B)\) using Bayes' Theorem, generates a contingency table scaled to a user‑defined population size, and produces three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations: a grouped bar chart, a heatmap, and a posterior probability bar plot. — bayes
code: bayes(   prevalence,   sensitivity,   false_positive,   index = 1000,   install_dependencies = TRUE )

title: Bayes' Theorem Calculator with Color‑Impaired‑Safe Visualizations Computes the posterior probability \(P(A|B)\) using Bayes' Theorem, generates a contingency table scaled to a user‑defined population size, and produces three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations: a grouped bar chart, a heatmap, and a posterior probability bar plot. — bayes
text: prevalence Prevalence \(P()\). Accepts numeric values percentage strings. sensitivity Sensitivity \(P(B|)\). Accepts numeric values percentage strings. false_positive False positive rate \(P(B|^-)\). Accepts numeric values percentage strings. index Population size used scale contingency table (default = 1000). install_dependencies Logical. TRUE, missing packages (dplyr, ggplot2, tidyr) installed automatically. FALSE, function stops informative error required package installed.

title: Bayes' Theorem Calculator with Color‑Impaired‑Safe Visualizations Computes the posterior probability \(P(A|B)\) using Bayes' Theorem, generates a contingency table scaled to a user‑defined population size, and produces three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations: a grouped bar chart, a heatmap, and a posterior probability bar plot. — bayes
text: named list containing: posterior_probability Posterior probability \(P(|B)\) rounded four decimals. contingency_table 3×3 data frame scaled counts. barchart Grouped bar chart test results condition (ggplot2 object). heatmap Heatmap contingency table (ggplot2 object). posterior_plot Posterior probability bar plot (ggplot2 object).

title: Bayes' Theorem Calculator with Color‑Impaired‑Safe Visualizations Computes the posterior probability \(P(A|B)\) using Bayes' Theorem, generates a contingency table scaled to a user‑defined population size, and produces three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations: a grouped bar chart, a heatmap, and a posterior probability bar plot. — bayes
text: function performs following steps: Validates converts probability inputs decimals. Computes \(P(B)\) using Law Total Probability Computes posterior probability using Bayes' Theorem. Constructs 3×3 contingency table : True Positives (TP) False Positives (FP) False Negatives (FN) True Negatives (TN) Generates three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations using Okabe–Ito palette.

title: Bayes' Theorem Calculator with Color‑Impaired‑Safe Visualizations Computes the posterior probability \(P(A|B)\) using Bayes' Theorem, generates a contingency table scaled to a user‑defined population size, and produces three color‑blind‑safe ggplot2 visualizations: a grouped bar chart, a heatmap, and a posterior probability bar plot. — bayes
code: # Basic usage bayes(7, 92, 7) #> $posterior_probability #> [1] 0.4973 #>  #> $contingency_table #>          Result Condition_Positive Condition_Negative  Total #> 1 Test Positive               64.4               65.1  129.5 #> 2 Test Negative                5.6              864.9  870.5 #> 3         Total               70.0              930.0 1000.0 #>  #> $barchart  #>  #> $heatmap  #>  #> $posterior_plot  #>   # Using decimal inputs bayes(0.07, 0.92, 0.07) #> $posterior_probability #> [1] 0.4973 #>  #> $contingency_table #>          Result Condition_Positive Condition_Negative  Total #> 1 Test Positive               64.4               65.1  129.5 #> 2 Test Negative                5.6              864.9  870.5 #> 3         Total               70.0              930.0 1000.0 #>  #> $barchart  #>  #> $heatmap  #>  #> $posterior_plot  #>   # Without automatic package installation if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ bayes(7, 92, 7, install_dependencies = FALSE) } # }
